items.reality: INFO is below the default WARNING threshold, so nothing appears until you lower the level (e.g. via basicConfig).
items.example: Fresh `python` → `import logging; logging.info('hi')` prints nothing. Add `logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)` first; now it shows up.

items.myth: logging is just a fancy print.
items.reality: It's a routing framework: one call can fan out to console, files and remote sinks, each with its own level and format.
items.example: Same `log.info('ingested %d', n)` can simultaneously: write JSON to stdout (for Datadog), append to a rotating file (for local debug), and POST to Sentry (only if WARNING+). Print can't.

items.reality: Libraries should only get a logger and emit; the application owns configuration. Libraries adding handlers causes duplicate/forced output.
items.example: Library `ragapp/chunker.py`: `log = logging.getLogger(__name__)`; emits only. Application `main.py`: calls `logging.basicConfig(...)` once. Don't have the library configure it — operators would lose control.
